title.en: Character Classes & Quantifiers: Building Real Patterns

description.en: Literal patterns only match exactly what you type. Character classes let a single position match a whole *set* of characters, and quantifiers let one piece repeat. Together they turn rigid strings into real patterns that match numbers, words, and structured text — and they introduce the most famous regex gotcha of all: greedy vs lazy matching.

story_variants.en: ## From Literals to Patterns A literal regex like `cat` matches exactly three characters in a row: `c`, then `a`, then `t`. Useful, but rigid. Real text isn't rigid — phone numbers vary, words have different lengths, IDs mix letters and digits. To match *shapes* instead of exact strings, you need two tools: **character classes** (match one of a *set* of characters) and **quantifiers** (let a piece *repeat*). Master these two and you can already match the vast majority of real-world patterns. ## Character Classes: One Slot, Many Options A character class uses square brackets to mean "match exactly one character from this set." - `[abc]` matches a single `a`, `b`, or `c`. - `[a-z]` uses a **range**: any one lowercase letter. `[0-9]` is any one digit. You can combine: `[a-z0-9]` is one lowercase letter *or* digit. - `[^abc]` is **negation** — the leading `^` means "any character that is *not* a, b, or c." Because some classes are so common, regex gives you **shorthand classes**: | Metacharacter | Meaning | |---|---| | `\d` | a digit, same as `[0-9]` | | `\D` | a non-digit, same as `[^0-9]` | | `\w` | a word character: letter, digit, or underscore | | `\W` | a non-word character | | `\s` | whitespace (space, tab, newline) | | `\S` | non-whitespace | | `.` | any character except newline | The uppercase versions are always the *negation* of the lowercase ones. And `.` is the wildcard — it matches almost anything, which makes it powerful and dangerous (more on that below). ## Quantifiers: Let It Repeat A character class matches **one** character. Quantifiers say *how many times* the thing before them may repeat: - `*` — zero or more (`a*` matches `''`, `'a'`, `'aaaa'`) - `+` — one or more (`a+` needs at least one `a`) - `?` — zero or one, i.e. optional (`colou?r` matches both `color` and `colour`) - `{n}` — exactly *n* times (`\d{4}` matches a 4-digit year) - `{n,m}` — between *n* and *m* times (`\d{2,4}` matches 2 to 4 digits) Now the classics fall out naturally. **Match a whole number:** `\d+` — one or more digits. **Match a word:** `\w+` — one or more word characters. **Match a price like 19.99:** `\d+\.\d{2}`. You're combining a class with a quantifier, and suddenly you're parsing real text. ## The Greedy Gotcha Here is the single most important trap in all of regex. By default, quantifiers are **greedy**: they grab as *much* as they possibly can while still letting the overall pattern match. Suppose you have the text `hi` and you write `<.*>` to match a tag. You expect ``. You get the *entire* string `hi` — because `.*` is greedy: it swallows everything up to the *last* `>` it can find, then backtracks just enough to succeed. The `.*` over-match is a bug that has bitten every regex user alive. The fix is a **lazy** (non-greedy) quantifier: add a `?` after it. `*?`, `+?`, and `??` grab as *little* as possible. So `<.*?>` matches just `` — it stops at the *first* `>`. > **Key idea:** Character classes (`[a-z]`, `\d`, `\w`, `\s`, `.`) decide *what* a single position can be; quantifiers (`*`, `+`, `?`, `{n,m}`) decide *how many times* it repeats. Quantifiers are **greedy** by default — they grab as much as possible, which is why `.*` over-matches — and adding `?` makes them **lazy**, grabbing as little as possible. ## Putting It Together With just these tools you can write `\d+` to pull every integer out of a log line, `\w+` to tokenize words, `[A-Z][a-z]+` to find capitalized names, or `#[0-9a-fA-F]{6}` to match a hex color. Character classes plus quantifiers are the workhorse of practical pattern matching — everything that follows (groups, anchors, lookarounds) is built on top of this foundation.

nodes.label: Classes (WHAT) + Quantifiers (HOW MANY)
children: greedy by default; add ? for lazy

right.label: Lazy <.*?> on 'hi' grabs as little as possible: ''

description.en: Greedy quantifiers swallow as much as they can then backtrack; adding ? makes them lazy and stop at the first match.

animation_sequence.code:
import re
text = "room 12, floor 3, 250 chairs"
nums = re.findall(r"\d+", text)
print(nums)

executionSteps.description: \d+ means one-or-more digits; findall scans left to right for every run.
Each maximal run of digits becomes one match — greedy + grabs the whole number.

output: Classes pick WHAT a slot matches: [a-z], \d, \w, \s, .
Greedy grabs as much as possible (.*) — add ? for lazy (.*?).

instruction.en: Use character classes and quantifiers to extract structured data. Write extract_ints(text) that returns a list of every integer in the string, in order, as Python ints. Use re.findall with the pattern \d+ (a digit class with the one-or-more quantifier) to grab each maximal run of digits, then convert each to int. With the given data, extract_ints('order 42: 7 boxes, 1000 units') must return [42, 7, 1000]. Expected output: [42, 7, 1000]

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import re

def extract_ints(text):
    # TODO: \d+ matches one or more digits (a whole number).
    # re.findall(r"\d+", text) returns a list of digit-run STRINGS.
    # Convert each match to an int and return the list.
    matches = re.findall(r"", text)  # fix the pattern
    return matches

print(extract_ints("order 42: 7 boxes, 1000 units"))

en: The pattern for 'one or more digits' is \d+ — the class \d is a single digit, and + means repeat one or more times.
re.findall(r"\d+", text) returns the matches as strings like ['42', '7', '1000'].
Because + is greedy, \d+ grabs the WHOLE number ('1000'), not one digit at a time — exactly what you want here.
Convert each string to int: return [int(m) for m in re.findall(r"\d+", text)].

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert extract_ints("order 42: 7 boxes, 1000 units") == [42, 7, 1000]
assert extract_ints("no digits here") == []
assert extract_ints("a1b22c333") == [1, 22, 333]
assert extract_ints("") == []
assert extract_ints("007 then 0") == [7, 0]
assert all(isinstance(n, int) for n in extract_ints("5 and 6"))

q.en: What does the character class [^a-z] match?

en: Exactly the three characters ^, a, and z
Any single character that is NOT a lowercase letter
Any lowercase letter, one or more times
The start of a line followed by a-z

q.en: Which pattern matches one or more digits (a whole number)?
On the text 'hi', what does the greedy pattern <.*> match?

en: Just '<b>', because it stops at the first >
The entire string 'hi', because .* grabs as much as possible
Nothing, because .* is invalid
Only 'hi' between the tags

q.en: How do you make a quantifier lazy (grab as little as possible)?

en: Put a ^ before it, like ^.*
Use {0} instead of *
Add a ? after it, like .*? or \d+?
Lazy matching is the default; you make it greedy with ?
